title: Adhesive on Cotton
stainChemistry: Adhesives on cotton split into four working classes: (1) rubber / contact — petroleum solvent (POG); (2) acrylic / white / school — warm NSD or IPA; (3) cyanoacrylate / super glue — acetone (cotton tolerates it); (4) hot-melt — freeze, pick, treat residue. Cotton is the most chemically durable common fiber — tolerates acetone, IPA, warm water, and most common solvents without damage.
whyThisWorks: Cotton's durability means the path is driven by adhesive class, not fiber sensitivity. Identify the glue first. Water-soluble glues give up to NSD + warm water. Acrylic adhesives lift with IPA. Cyanoacrylate needs acetone. Rubber cement breaks under petroleum solvents. Cotton handles all of these at room temperature; heat is the only thing to avoid until residue is fully gone.
defaultAssumption: Assume a water-soluble or acrylic adhesive unless the user says otherwise — that's the majority case. Cyanoacrylate gets called out explicitly if they mention super glue.

spottingProtocol.instruction: Ask: what kind? Super glue → acetone. Rubber cement → POG. White / school / acrylic → IPA or NSD. Hot-melt → chill, pick, treat residue. Remove pickable bulk with a dull blade before anything wet hits it.
Warm water flush from the back of the fabric. Cotton tolerates warm; water-soluble glues loosen fast.
NSD directly on the spot. Tamp with a bristle brush. Water-soluble adhesives lift here.

Residue remains: IPA on a white cloth for acrylic / white glue. Acetone on a cotton swab for confirmed cyanoacrylate — cotton tolerates acetone, unlike acetate blends. Tamp, don't pour. Flip cloth frequently.
Flush warm, blot, inspect. Air-dry only — heat sets residual adhesive into cotton fiber permanently.
Repeat steps 3–5 once if residue persists. If the stain has been through a dryer or iron, escalate — heat-set adhesive on cotton usually needs pro chemistry.

steps: 1. ID adhesive class. Scrape pickable bulk with dull blade.
4. If residue remains: IPA (acrylic/white) or acetone (super glue) on white cloth. Tamp, not pour.
5. Flush warm. Blot. Air-dry only — no heat until clean.
professionalProtocol.temperature: room temperature through spotting — no heat drying until residue is gone
warnings: Never heat an uncured or residual adhesive — it cures into the fiber permanently.
Test every solvent on an inside seam on dyed or printed cotton.
Never pour solvent — tamp with a cloth for placement control.

homeSolutions.instruction: Different glues want different removers. Super glue? Acetone-based nail polish remover — cotton handles it. School / white glue? Warm water and dish soap. Rubber cement or contact cement? Goo Gone or orange oil. Hot glue? Freeze and pick off. Skip this step and you'll guess wrong.

Before wetting anything, scrape the raised or rubbery bulk off with the edge of a credit card or a dull butter knife. Don't pull hard on the fabric — if it doesn't come free easily, leave it for the solvent step.

homeSolutions.agent: White / School Glue → Warm Water + Dawn
homeSolutions.instruction: Water-soluble glues give up fast on cotton. Run warm water through the back of the stain for 60 seconds. Work a drop of Dawn into the spot with a fingertip. Let it sit 5 minutes, rinse. Two passes clears most of it.

homeSolutions.agent: Super Glue → Acetone on Cotton Swab
homeSolutions.instruction: Cotton tolerates acetone-based nail polish remover well. Dab a cotton swab from the BACK of the fabric. Tamp-and-blot, don't pour. Super glue dissolves in seconds. Flush cold water right after.

homeSolutions.agent: Rubber Cement / Contact Glue → Citrus or Oil
homeSolutions.instruction: Apply Goo Gone, orange oil, or cooking oil to the residue. Let it sit 10 minutes — the adhesive gets gummy. Work gently with a soft toothbrush, lift dissolved residue with a paper towel. Wash the oil out with Dawn + warm water.
Put the garment in the freezer 20 minutes. Hot glue turns brittle when frozen and pops off with light pressure from a dull blade. Any film left behind runs through the Dawn + warm water pass.

Air-dry fully before the dryer. Adhesive residue cures permanently under dryer heat. Inspect in daylight — not the laundry-room bulb — before running hot. If you still see or feel a tacky spot, repeat the solvent step that matched your glue before drying.

steps: 1. ID the glue: super glue, white/school, rubber cement, hot glue — each has its own solvent.
2. Scrape off bulk with a credit card edge.
3. Super glue: acetone nail polish remover on a cotton swab from the back.
4. White / school glue: warm water + Dawn, work in, rinse.
5. Rubber / contact: Goo Gone or orange oil, dwell 10 min, soft-brush, wash out with Dawn.
6. Hot glue: freeze, pick, clean residue with Dawn + warm water.
7. Air-dry fully before the dryer — heat cures residual adhesive permanently.
products: A dull blade or credit card
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the adhesive has been through a dryer or iron, or two full rounds haven't improved it. Take it to a dry cleaner with the adhesive type identified.
warnings: No heat until residue is gone — cures adhesive permanently into cotton
Test solvents on an inside seam first on printed or colored cotton
Cotton tolerates acetone; avoid acetone on cotton/acetate blends

neverDo: Never heat / iron / dry an uncleaned adhesive — cures it into the fiber permanently
Never pour solvent — tamp with a cloth for placement control
Never use acetone if the label says acetate or rayon blend
fiberSensitivities: Cotton is among the most solvent-tolerant common fibers — acetone, IPA, POG all safe
Heat is the only real restriction until residue is fully gone
Colored / printed cotton: test solvents on an inside seam first

materialWarnings: No heat until residue is fully gone
Verify fiber content — acetone on acetate blends is destructive
Test all solvents on an inside seam first on colored/printed cotton

escalation.when: If the adhesive has been heat-set, ironed / dried with residue present, or two home rounds haven't cleared it — take it to a dry cleaner.
escalation.whatToTell: Name the glue class: "super glue / rubber cement / school glue / hot glue on cotton, treated with [solvent] — no heat applied." Gives the pro the solvent class to reach for.

contextQuestions: What kind of glue — super glue, school glue, rubber cement, hot glue, or something else?
Has the fabric been through a dryer or iron since the glue hit it?
What's the fiber blend? (Pure cotton tolerates acetone; acetate blends do not.)
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Adhesive class decides the solvent path. Cotton is forgiving on solvent choice — the main restrictions are heat (before residue is gone) and acetone on acetate blends.
